---
title: "Birefringence in the A=1 lattice — a finding from the Dirac derivation"
description: >-
  How directional dependence in the lattice's propagation showed up
  as a side effect of deriving the Dirac equation, and what it
  implies for what we can predict and measure.
author: "Jack D. Menendez"
date: "2026-05-20"
draft: true
categories: [essay, Paper I, Dirac, birefringence, anisotropy]
---

<!--
Drafting notes (delete before publishing):

- Stage: drafts/ directory. Excluded from the live site.
- When ready, move the directory to essays/posts/ (preserve the
  date-prefixed slug) and set `draft: false` (or remove the field).
- Co-locate figures in this directory; reference them by bare
  filename (e.g. ![caption](fig-foo.png){fig-alt="..."}).
- Suggested figures (candidates from c:/dev/dcl/figures/):
    - induced_action_ellipsoid.png (the dual-anisotropy figure)
    - frame_matrix_ellipsoid.png (frame matrix as quadratic form)
    - dirac_cones_overlay.png (Arnold tongue / Dirac crossings)
  Copy whichever fits into this directory and rename to a
  fig-*.png convention.
-->

## The result in one paragraph

> *TODO:* state the finding in one paragraph. What does
> birefringence look like *on the bipartite octahedral lattice*?
> Specifically: which physical quantity has direction-dependent
> propagation, what sets the anisotropy axis, and how strong is
> the effect at the lattice scale. Make this the load-bearing
> sentence — everything below should support it.

## How it came up

> *TODO:* which step of the Dirac derivation surfaced it.  The
> birefringence did not appear because we set out to find it; it
> emerged as a side-condition of demanding that the discrete
> propagator reduce to the Dirac operator in the continuum limit.
> Name the section in Paper~I, the equation number, and the
> moment in the derivation where the anisotropy became
> unavoidable.

## What it looks like

### The math

> *TODO:* the form of the induced anisotropy.  This is the place
> to put the actual quadratic form / dispersion relation /
> eigenstructure that captures the effect.  Lead with the
> coordinate-free statement (e.g. "the kinematic and gauge
> sectors share the same optical axis $V_1+V_2+V_3$ but with
> eigenvalues $\{4,4,1\}$ vs. $\{4,4,16\}$"), then unpack.

### The picture

> *TODO:* a figure showing the anisotropy.  The
> `induced_action_ellipsoid` figure from Paper~I shows the
> kinematic / gauge ellipsoid split directly and is a natural
> candidate to reuse here.  Drop a copy into this directory and
> reference it below.

<!-- Example image reference (replace with real figure once added):

![Dual anisotropy of the kinematic and gauge sectors: prolate ellipsoid (M, eigenvalues {4,4,1}) vs. oblate ellipsoid (Q, eigenvalues {4,4,16}), sharing the optical axis $V_1+V_2+V_3$.](fig-dual-anisotropy.png){fig-alt="Two 3D ellipsoid plots side by side. Left: prolate ellipsoid (long axis along the diagonal $V_1+V_2+V_3 = (1,1,-1)$) coloured with a red-to-blue scale showing dispersion. Right: oblate ellipsoid (short axis along the same diagonal) showing the gauge-sector quadratic form."}

-->

## Why it matters

> *TODO:* connect back to the framework's load-bearing claims.
>
> - Which audit row in Paper~I does this support / refine?  Cite
>   the row.
> - Does the anisotropy survive O$_h$-averaging in the continuum
>   limit (the test of whether it's a discreteness artefact or a
>   real prediction)?  Note the answer either way.
> - What downstream subprojects inherit it?  Paper~II (gauge
>   derivation) and the discrete-probability paper both consume
>   the dual-anisotropy structure — name the consumers.

## Open / next

> *TODO:* what's unresolved and what would falsify the claim.
>
> - Is the magnitude of the anisotropy fixed by the substrate
>   alone, or does it pick up corrections from the conservation
>   axiom?
> - What observational signature, if any, would distinguish
>   lattice-induced birefringence from continuum birefringence
>   in a real material?
> - Is there an `exp_NN` script that lets a reader reproduce the
>   anisotropy diagnostic numerically?  Link it.

## References

- Paper~I — *Geometry First*, §<section>, equation <eqn>.
  doi:[10.5281/zenodo.20078529](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20078529)
- Working notes in
  [`external/dcl/notes/<note>.md`](https://github.com/JackDMenendez/dcl/tree/main/notes)
  *(fill in the specific note that captures the derivation
  step)*.
